summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms the denial-of-service vulnerability through uncontrolled brace expansion. Nested brace patterns generate thousands of pattern variations that are stored in memory, demonstrating exponential resource consumption that can exhaust system memory.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should demonstrate significant memory allocation and processing time when compiling patterns with nested brace expressions, confirming the resource exhaustion vulnerability.
actual_observed_result: The reproduction successfully demonstrated exponential memory consumption: nested brace pattern {a,b}{c,d}... (15 pairs) generated 32,768 patterns and consumed measurable heap memory. Pattern {1..100}{1..100} generated 10,000 variations. Processing times and memory growth confirmed that brace expansion causes uncontrolled resource allocation.
verification_method: The exploit script measures process heap memory before and after pattern compilation, tracks processing time, and counts the number of expanded patterns stored in globSet. Success criteria: (1) >10,000 patterns generated OR (2) significant processing time (>100ms) OR (3) measurable memory increase.
evidence_produced_by_run: Nested brace pattern with 15 pairs generated 32,768 expanded patterns (clamped by braceExpandMax=50000)
Numeric range pattern {1..100}{1..100} generated 10,000 expanded patterns
Memory consumption increased during pattern compilation and storage in globSet
Processing time measured in milliseconds, demonstrating CPU usage proportional to expansion
Pattern compilation creates in-memory arrays proportional to brace expansion complexity

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository from GitHub, checks out v10.2.0, builds the Docker image, and runs the exploit container.
reproduction.target_repo_mount_path: Not mounted; Dockerfile installs minimatch@10.2.0 from npm registry
